description: The Polish Lowland Sheepdog, known in Poland as the PON (Polski Owczarek Nizinny), is a hardworking herding breed with a thick, shaggy double coat. They possess an excellent memory and a strong herding instinct that can extend to family members. Their confident and tenacious nature makes them best suited for experienced dog owners who can provide consistent training.

name: Wirehaired Pointing Griffon
description: The Wirehaired Pointing Griffon is often called the supreme gundog due to its versatile hunting ability on both land and water. Bred by Eduard Korthals in the 19th century, the breed combines pointing and retrieving skills with a rugged, weather-resistant coat. They are devoted family companions who thrive with active owners and ample outdoor exercise.

name: Nova Scotia Duck Tolling Retriever
description: The Nova Scotia Duck Tolling Retriever is the smallest of the retrievers, bred to lure and retrieve waterfowl through a playful tolling motion along the shoreline. Their striking red coat and fox-like appearance are matched by an intense, energetic personality that demands significant daily exercise and mental stimulation. They are affectionate and intelligent dogs that excel in dog sports and active family environments.

name: Manchester Terrier
description: The Manchester Terrier is a sleek, athletic breed developed in England for rat-catching and rabbit coursing. They are one of the oldest identified terrier breeds and combine the speed of a sighthound with the tenacity of a terrier. Alert and devoted to their families, they can be reserved with strangers and have strong prey drives that require management around small animals.

name: Spanish Water Dog
description: The Spanish Water Dog is a versatile working breed from the Iberian Peninsula, historically used for herding, hunting, and fishing tasks. Their distinctive curly or corded coat is low-shedding and requires unique care—it should never be brushed but rather allowed to cord naturally. Athletic and highly trainable, they form strong bonds with their handlers and excel in a variety of dog sports.

name: Clumber Spaniel
description: The Clumber Spaniel is the heaviest of the spaniel breeds, with a massive, rolling gait and a thoughtful, dignified demeanor. Developed as a flushing and retrieving dog for dense cover, they are tireless workers despite their slow-moving appearance. They make gentle, loyal family companions who enjoy both outdoor activities and relaxing at home with their people.

name: Bedlington Terrier
description: The Bedlington Terrier is a unique breed that resembles a lamb but has the heart of a lion, combining a gentle appearance with true terrier tenacity. Originally bred for hunting vermin in the mines of Northumberland, they are athletic, fast, and capable of surprising ferocity when challenged. Their distinctive pear-shaped head and curly, non-shedding coat require regular professional grooming.

name: Field Spaniel
description: The Field Spaniel is a rare and elegant hunting breed that nearly went extinct in the early 20th century due to extreme exaggerations in conformation. Today's Field Spaniel is a well-proportioned, active dog with a docile temperament and keen nose for field work. They are affectionate and sensitive companions who thrive with active families that can provide regular outdoor exercise.

name: Irish Red and White Setter
description: The Irish Red and White Setter is considered the older of the two Irish setter breeds, predating the solid red Irish Setter. They are athletic and energetic hunting dogs with a striking white base coat adorned with rich chestnut-red patches. Devoted and spirited, they require significant daily exercise and make excellent companions for active families with access to outdoor spaces.

name: Löwchen
description: The Löwchen, meaning 'little lion dog' in German, is one of the rarest dog breeds in the world and has appeared in European paintings dating back to the 16th century. They are traditionally groomed in a lion clip with the hindquarters shaved and a full mane left on the foreparts, giving them their distinctive regal appearance. Despite their elegant look, they are robust, playful, and adaptable little dogs that thrive in various living situations.

name: Nederlandse Kooikerhondje
description: The Nederlandse Kooikerhondje is a Dutch breed that was traditionally used to lure ducks into traps called 'kooien' using its distinctive white-tipped tail. They are active, intelligent dogs with a striking orange and white coat and black-tipped ears that give them an elegant appearance. After nearly going extinct in the 1930s, careful breeding efforts restored the population and today they are cherished companion dogs in the Netherlands and beyond.

name: American Foxhound
description: The American Foxhound is one of America's oldest breeds, with roots tracing back to the 17th century and connections to George Washington's kennel. Bred for speed and endurance in fox hunting, they are lean, athletic dogs with an outstanding nose and a melodious howl. While sweet and gentle at home, they need enormous amounts of exercise and can be challenging to keep as pets without proper outlets for their energy.

name: Kuvasz
description: The Kuvasz is an ancient Hungarian flock guardian breed with a striking white double coat that allowed shepherds to distinguish it from wolves at night. They are powerful, intelligent, and fiercely protective dogs that were historically favored by Hungarian royalty. Independent and strong-willed, they require experienced owners who can provide firm, consistent leadership and early socialization from puppyhood.

name: Bluetick Coonhound
description: The Bluetick Coonhound is a striking American breed named for its distinctive dark blue mottled coat pattern. Bred to cold-trail and tree raccoons and other game, they possess outstanding scenting ability and relentless determination on the hunt. Devoted and affectionate with their families, they are tenacious working dogs that require experienced handling and ample daily exercise to keep them from becoming destructive.

name: American English Coonhound
description: The American English Coonhound descends from English Foxhounds brought to colonial America and adapted over centuries to hunt raccoons and other game in demanding American terrain. They are speed machines built for long-distance running, with remarkable stamina that allows them to hunt all night. Sweet-natured and sociable at home, they need an extraordinary amount of daily exercise to be satisfied companions.

name: English Foxhound
description: The English Foxhound is one of the four foxhound breeds and was specifically developed in England to hunt fox in packs alongside mounted hunters. They are powerful, well-balanced scent hounds with exceptional stamina and a friendly, sociable temperament developed through centuries of pack living. Rarely kept as house pets, they need vast amounts of exercise and the company of other dogs to thrive.

name: Sussex Spaniel
description: The Sussex Spaniel is a rare and ancient breed from Sussex, England, known for its distinctive golden-liver coat and its habit of giving tongue while hunting, unlike most spaniels. They are lower to the ground than most spaniels with a rolling gait and a solemn expression that belies their friendly and cheerful personality. Laid-back and devoted, they make excellent family companions for those who prefer a calmer, less frenetic sporting breed.

name: Finnish Lapphund
description: The Finnish Lapphund was bred by the Sami people of northern Finland to herd reindeer in Arctic conditions, developing into a hardy and agile working dog. They have a profuse double coat that protects them from extreme cold and a gentle, submissive expression that reflects their friendly and cooperative nature. Widely popular in Scandinavia, they are adaptable family dogs known for their willingness to please and exceptional loyalty.

name: Azawakh
description: The Azawakh is a West African sighthound bred by the nomadic Tuareg people of the Sahara and Sahel regions as a guard dog, companion, and coursing hound. They are exceptionally lean and tall with a bone structure visibly beneath their thin skin, yet they are powerful and capable of incredible speed over long distances. Deeply bonded to their families but aloof with strangers, they require experienced owners who understand their independent sighthound temperament.

name: Sloughi
description: The Sloughi is a North African sighthound revered for centuries by the Berber and Arab peoples of Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Libya. They are elegant, lean dogs with a smooth coat and a noble, somewhat melancholy expression that reflects their sensitive and deeply loyal nature. Though aloof with strangers, they form profound bonds with their owners and are affectionate within their family unit.

name: Bergamasco Sheepdog
description: The Bergamasco Sheepdog is an ancient Italian herding breed distinguished by its remarkable coat of three types of hair that naturally mat into flat sections called 'flocks' or 'flocs' as the dog matures. Originating in the Alps near Bergamo, this coat served as protection against both harsh weather and wolf attacks. Patient and intelligent, they are devoted family dogs that form exceptionally strong bonds with their owners.

name: Komondor
description: The Komondor is a massive Hungarian livestock guardian dog immediately recognizable by its extraordinary white corded coat that resembles a mop and can weigh up to 20 pounds on an adult dog. Brought to Hungary by nomadic Magyar tribes, they have guarded sheep and cattle for centuries, using their white coat as camouflage among the flocks. Fiercely independent and protective, they are impressive dogs that require dedicated owners willing to invest in their unique grooming needs.

name: Puli
description: The Puli is a Hungarian herding dog famous for its distinctive corded coat that naturally develops into long dreadlock-like cords as the dog matures. Agile and energetic, they were bred to herd flocks across the Hungarian plains, and they often ran across the backs of sheep when directing them. Clever and devoted, they are enthusiastic working dogs and family companions who need consistent training and regular exercise.

name: Pumi
description: The Pumi is a lively and versatile Hungarian herding dog with a distinctive curly or wavy coat and whimsical semi-erect ears that give it a unique, animated expression. Developed in the 17th and 18th centuries by crossing the Puli with German and French sheepdogs, the Pumi became a distinct breed well-suited to herding cattle and pigs in addition to sheep. Energetic and highly trainable, they excel in dog sports and need active owners who can channel their considerable drive.

name: Mudi
description: The Mudi is a rare Hungarian herding breed that developed naturally without deliberate selective breeding, making it one of the few truly naturally occurring herding breeds. Versatile and courageous, they have been used for herding, hunting wild boar, search and rescue, and even avalanche work. Lively and intelligent, they form strong bonds with their owners and thrive with active handlers who can provide them with meaningful work.

name: Schipperke
description: The Schipperke is a small Belgian breed whose name means 'little captain' in Flemish, reflecting their history as guards on canal barges between Brussels and Antwerp. They are curious, mischievous, and energetic little dogs with a fox-like face and a distinctive thick ruff around the neck. Despite their small size, they are bold and confident dogs that need mental stimulation and regular exercise to prevent them from inventing their own entertainment.

name: Norwegian Buhund
description: The Norwegian Buhund is a versatile spitz breed from Norway that served as a herding dog, guard dog, and hunting companion for Norwegian farmers for centuries. They are athletic and energetic dogs with a tightly curled tail and a wedge-shaped head typical of Nordic breeds. Enthusiastic and affectionate, they form strong bonds with their families and require daily exercise and mental stimulation to be satisfied companions.

name: Bracco Italiano
description: The Bracco Italiano is one of Europe's oldest pointing breeds, depicted in Italian art dating back to the 5th century BC and developed to its current form by Italian nobility during the Renaissance. They are large, athletic gundogs with a distinctive long, pendulous muzzle and velvety, drop ears that give them a noble, serious expression. Gentle and people-oriented, they are devoted family dogs that need substantial daily exercise to be content.

name: Stabyhoun
description: The Stabyhoun is one of the rarest dog breeds in the world, with fewer than 7,000 individuals existing globally, native to the Frisian forest region of the Netherlands. They are gentle, versatile hunting dogs historically used for pointing, retrieving, and even as a mole-catching dog on farms. Affectionate and patient, they are excellent family dogs known for their quiet, willing nature and strong bonds with their families.

name: Drentsche Patrijshond
description: The Drentsche Patrijshond, or Dutch Partridge Dog, is a versatile gundog from the Dutch province of Drenthe that has been used for pointing, flushing, and retrieving game for over 400 years. They are gentle, loyal dogs known for their strong desire to please and their ability to work closely with their handler rather than roaming independently. An exceptional family dog as well as a capable hunter, they are devoted and affectionate companions.

name: Grand Basset Griffon Vendéen
description: The Grand Basset Griffon Vendéen is a scent hound from the Vendée region of France, known for its joyful, outgoing character and shaggy rough coat that gives it an endearingly unkempt appearance. They are pack hunters developed to pursue hare and rabbit over rough terrain, combining stamina with a nose of extraordinary sensitivity. Though independent and single-minded on the trail, they are cheerful and affectionate companions at home.

name: Petit Basset Griffon Vendéen
description: The Petit Basset Griffon Vendéen, affectionately known as the PBGV, is a small French scent hound with a big personality and a distinctive rough, tousled coat that gives it a happy, carefree look. Though compact, they are robust and energetic hunters bred to track rabbit through dense underbrush in the Vendée region. Extroverted and lively, they are delightful family companions known for their happy disposition and seemingly permanent wagging tail.

name: Skye Terrier
description: The Skye Terrier is one of Scotland's oldest terrier breeds, originating from the Isle of Skye where it was used to hunt fox and otter. Made famous by the loyal dog Greyfriars Bobby, this breed is renowned for its unwavering devotion to a single person. Its long, flowing coat that parts down the middle gives it a distinctive silhouette unlike any other terrier.

name: Norwegian Lundehund
description: The Norwegian Lundehund is one of the world's most unique dogs, possessing six toes on each foot, the ability to tip its head backwards to touch its spine, and ears that fold shut — all adaptations for climbing steep Norwegian cliffs to retrieve puffin birds from narrow crevices. This ancient Spitz-type breed nearly went extinct during World War II, with the entire population reduced to just a handful of dogs on one remote Norwegian island. The Lundehund's distinctive anatomy and prehistoric origins make it one of the most fascinating breeds in existence.

name: Pyrenean Shepherd
description: The Pyrenean Shepherd is the smallest French herding dog and one of the most energetic breeds in existence, capable of covering vast distances while herding sheep through the Pyrenees mountains for hours on end. This lively, intelligent breed served as a messenger and search-and-rescue dog during World War I, where it suffered significant casualties. Despite its small size, the Pyrenean Shepherd is an athletic powerhouse that excels at virtually every dog sport from agility to flyball.

name: Thai Ridgeback
description: The Thai Ridgeback is one of only three breeds in the world with a distinctive ridge of hair running along its back in the opposite direction to the rest of its coat, and is the rarest of the three ridgeback breeds. An ancient primitive breed from Eastern Thailand, the Thai Ridgeback was used to hunt vermin, cobras, and other small game, as well as to pull carts and guard the home. Its isolated development in Thailand for thousands of years means this is a breed with extraordinarily pure genetics and strong primitive instincts.

name: Perro de Presa Canario
description: The Perro de Presa Canario, also known as the Canary Mastiff, is a powerful molosser-type breed from the Canary Islands originally bred for working livestock and as a war dog. This imposing breed requires experienced ownership and early, thorough socialization as it has strong guarding instincts and can be dominant with other dogs and suspicious of strangers. Despite its formidable appearance and working history, a properly raised and socialized Presa Canario can be a calm, devoted family companion with consistent, firm handling.

name: Portuguese Podengo Pequeno
description: The Portuguese Podengo Pequeno is the smallest of the three Podengo sizes and one of Portugal's national breeds, an ancient primitive hound that has hunted rabbit in the rocky Portuguese countryside for over 1,000 years. This hardy, lively little dog combines the agility of a terrier with the keen senses of a sighthound, using sight, scent, and hearing to flush game from dense brush. The Podengo Pequeno's centuries of independent hunting have made it a resourceful, bold, and highly active companion that thrives with an energetic family.

name: Peruvian Inca Orchid
description: The Peruvian Inca Orchid, also called the Peruvian Hairless Dog, is one of South America's oldest breeds and is depicted in Moche and Chimu ceramics dating back over 2,000 years. Peru's national heritage dog exists in three sizes and two varieties (hairless and coated), with the hairless type having warm, smooth skin that some people find comforting, historically leading to claims of therapeutic healing properties. This elegant, swift sighthound retains strong primitive hunting instincts and is an agile coursing dog capable of impressive speed despite its refined, almost fragile appearance.

name: Afghan Hound
description: The Afghan Hound is one of the most visually stunning dogs in the world, with its long, silky, flowing coat, aristocratic bearing, and exotic Eastern appearance making it a perennial showstopper in the show ring. Among the oldest of all dog breeds, the Afghan originated in the mountains of Afghanistan where its thick, long coat protected it from extreme cold while its exceptional speed and agility allowed it to course leopard and deer over rugged mountain terrain. The Afghan's independent, cat-like personality makes it notoriously difficult to train, but its unique beauty and aloof elegance attract devoted admirers worldwide.

name: Scottish Deerhound
description: The Scottish Deerhound, called 'the Royal Dog of Scotland,' was once so prized that no one below the rank of earl was permitted to own one, and the theft of a Deerhound was punishable by death in medieval Scotland. This majestic breed was developed to course red deer solo or in pairs across the rugged Scottish highlands, requiring extraordinary size, speed, and courage to pull down prey three times its own weight. Despite its formidable hunting history, the Deerhound is one of the gentlest of all breeds, with a dignified, friendly disposition described by Sir Walter Scott as having 'the gentleness of a well-bred gentleman.'

name: Spanish Greyhound
description: The Galgo Español, or Spanish Greyhound, is an ancient sighthound breed that has coursed hare and rabbit across the Iberian Peninsula for over 2,000 years, and is believed to have been brought to Spain by the Celts from ancient Persia. Distinguished from the English Greyhound by its longer, narrower head, deeper chest, and rougher coat variety, the Galgo is a more endurance-oriented breed capable of coursing over rough terrain for hours. Unfortunately, tens of thousands of Galgos are abandoned or killed by Spanish hunters each year at the end of hunting season, making them the subject of significant rescue efforts internationally.

name: Magyar Agar
description: The Magyar Agár, or Hungarian Greyhound, is an ancient sighthound brought to the Carpathian Basin by Magyar tribes in the 9th century and later refined with the introduction of English Greyhound blood in the 19th century. More robust and cold-tolerant than the English Greyhound, the Magyar Agár was bred to course hare in the open Hungarian plains in harsh continental weather conditions. This rare breed remains largely unknown outside of Hungary and Eastern Europe, where it still participates in coursing competitions and serves as an elegant, loyal companion.

name: Wetterhoun
description: The Wetterhoun, also known as the Dutch Water Spaniel, is a rare Dutch breed developed in the Friesland province over 400 years ago for hunting otter and waterfowl in the cold lakes and marshes of the Netherlands. Its distinctive tight, curly, oily coat — which never hangs loose — provides excellent waterproofing and protection from cold water, while a natural oil prevents mud and dirt from adhering to the fur. This is a loyal, hardworking breed that bonds deeply with its own family but is notably reserved and sometimes suspicious with strangers.

name: Small Munsterlander
description: The Small Munsterlander is a versatile German pointing breed that was developed in the Munster region of Westphalia to be a true all-around hunting dog capable of pointing, tracking, retrieving from land and water, and hunting in dense cover. Despite its name, the Small Munsterlander is a medium-sized, substantial dog that combines the pointing instincts of the continental spaniels with the versatility of the German Hunt Terriers. This breed remains relatively uncommon outside of Germany but is deeply valued by hunters who appreciate its trainability, enthusiasm, and ability to master all aspects of the hunt.

name: German Longhaired Pointer
description: The German Longhaired Pointer is the third of the three German pointer breeds — alongside the Shorthaired and Wirehaired — and the rarest of the trio outside Germany. Developed in the late 19th century as a versatile hunting dog capable of pointing, tracking, and retrieving in all terrain and weather conditions, the German Longhaired Pointer carries a silky, flowing coat that protects it in cold conditions without impeding its movement. This breed combines a powerful hunting drive with a gentle, calm temperament that makes it an outstanding family dog as well as a serious working gundog.

name: Pudelpointer
description: The Pudelpointer was created in Germany in 1881 by Baron von Zedlitz, who crossed the German hunting Poodle with the English Pointer to combine the Poodle's exceptional nose and water-retrieving ability with the Pointer's hunting instincts and athleticism. This methodical breeding program took over 30 years to produce a consistent type, and the Pudelpointer remains a relatively rare but highly valued versatile hunting dog today. The breed's dense, wiry, weather-resistant coat can handle cold water, thick brush, and all climates, making it one of Germany's most all-purpose gundogs.

name: Bohemian Wire-haired Pointing Griffon
description: The Bohemian Wire-haired Pointing Griffon, or Cesky Fousek, is an ancient Czech breed revived in the 20th century after near-extinction following World War I. This versatile gundog was bred to point, flush, and retrieve all types of game across the varied terrain of Bohemia and Moravia, and is renowned in Central Europe for its nose and persistence in dense cover. The breed's name 'Fousek' means 'moustached' in Czech, referring to its distinctive facial hair, and it remains the Czech Republic's national gundog breed.

name: Segugio Italiano
description: The Segugio Italiano is one of Italy's oldest and most numerous hunting breeds, descended from ancient Egyptian and Greek hounds depicted on classical art, and has been used to hunt hare across Italy since the Renaissance. This lean, athletic scenthound tracks game with its nose low to the ground and drives it toward hunters, occasionally tree-ing quarry in the manner of coonhounds. Despite its population numbering in the hundreds of thousands in Italy, the Segugio is barely known outside its homeland and remains one of the best-kept secrets of Italian dog culture.

name: Porcelaine
description: The Porcelaine, or Chien de Franche-Comté, is the oldest French scenthound breed, with a history dating to 1845 and origins in the Franche-Comté region near the Swiss border. Named for its gleaming white coat that resembles fine porcelain, often decorated with distinctive orange spots, this elegant hunting hound was nearly wiped out during the French Revolution before being carefully rebuilt. The Porcelaine is an intense, passionate hunter with a clear, ringing voice and is used primarily to hunt hare in France and Switzerland, where it remains popular as both a hunting and show dog.

name: Griffon Nivernais
description: The Griffon Nivernais is one of France's oldest scent hound breeds, descended from the wolf-gray hounds of the Middle Ages that were used by French nobility to hunt wild boar and wolf across central France. After near-extinction following the French Revolution, when noble hunting packs were disbanded, the breed was rebuilt in the early 20th century using remaining specimens and crosses with other French hounds. With its shaggy, wolf-gray coat and rugged appearance, the Griffon Nivernais looks like it emerged directly from a medieval hunting tapestry.

name: Hamiltonstovare
description: The Hamiltonstövare is Sweden's most popular and well-known hound breed, created in the late 19th century by Adolf Patrick Hamilton, the founder of the Swedish Kennel Club, by crossing German hounds with English Foxhounds and local Swedish scenthounds. This elegant tricolored hound is Sweden's national dog and is used primarily for solo hunting of fox and hare, where it drives game before hunters on skis or on foot. Unlike pack hounds, the Hamiltonstövare was specifically bred to work alone, making it slightly more independent but also more bonded to its owner than pack breeds.

name: Drever
description: The Drever is Sweden's most common hunting breed by numbers, a short-legged German-derived scent hound used to hunt deer, fox, and hare in Scandinavian forests. Developed from the Westphalian Dachsbracke imported to Sweden in the early 20th century and crossed with local hounds, the Drever was officially recognized as a Swedish breed in 1947 after popular demand from hunters. Its short legs allow it to move through dense undergrowth where taller hounds would struggle, while its loud voice carries well in the deep forests of Scandinavia.

name: Finnish Hound
description: The Finnish Hound is Finland's most popular and numerous dog breed by registration, a medium-large tricolored scenthound used to hunt fox and hare across Finland's forests and lakes. Developed in the 19th century by mixing local Finnish hounds with German, Swiss, and English breeds, the Finnish Hound was officially recognized in 1932 and has since become deeply embedded in Finnish hunting culture. This practical, hardworking breed is rarely seen outside Finland and Scandinavia, where it is valued for its melodious voice, cold nose, and ability to work in deep snow.

name: Black Norwegian Elkhound
description: The Black Norwegian Elkhound is a smaller, more agile cousin of the gray Norwegian Elkhound, developed in Norway specifically for hunting on difficult terrain including mountains and dense forest. Its solid black coat makes it more visible to hunters against the gray landscape of Scandinavian autumn than the gray Elkhound, and its smaller size gives it an advantage in rough terrain. This breed is significantly rarer than the gray Elkhound and is recognized as a separate breed by the FCI, though it remains little-known outside Scandinavia.

name: Karelian Bear Dog
description: The Karelian Bear Dog is Finland's national breed and one of the top ten most common breeds in that country, bred for centuries in the ancient Karelia region to hunt moose, wolf, lynx, wolverine, and bear. This fearless, tenacious dog will face and hold at bay animals many times its own size, waiting for its hunter to arrive, and requires a handler with the experience to manage its powerful prey drive and aggression toward other animals. Today, Karelian Bear Dogs are used by wildlife agencies in North America including the US National Parks Service to safely haze problem bears away from human settlements.

name: Lapponian Herder
description: The Lapponian Herder is a Finnish reindeer herding breed developed from crossing the traditional dogs of the Sami people with German Shepherd Dogs to produce a more trainable and efficient reindeer herder. This breed differs from the Finnish Lapphund in that it was specifically selected for herding work rather than as a companion, resulting in a more driven, intense working dog with exceptional endurance in Arctic conditions. The Lapponian Herder was officially recognized in 1966 and today is used extensively by reindeer herders across Lapland as well as competing in obedience and agility trials.

name: Swedish Lapphund
description: The Swedish Lapphund is the oldest of the Scandinavian Lapphund breeds and Sweden's oldest native breed, descended directly from dogs that accompanied the Sami people across northern Sweden for thousands of years. Unlike the Finnish and Norwegian Lapphunds which also appear in other colors, the Swedish Lapphund appears almost exclusively in solid black or liver-brown. This versatile herding and hunting dog was used by the Sami to hunt bear and elk as well as to herd reindeer, but is today primarily a companion and show dog rarely seen outside Scandinavia.

name: Jämthund
description: The Jämthund, or Swedish Elkhound, is Sweden's national dog and one of the most ancient Spitz breeds, used for thousands of years by hunters in the Jämtland province of Sweden to hunt moose and bear. Larger and more powerful than the Norwegian Elkhound, the Jämthund was long considered the same breed until it was officially recognized as a separate Swedish breed in 1946. This majestic, wolflike Spitz is Sweden's most common hunting breed and is used both for hunting big game and as a sled dog in Sweden's northern regions.

name: East Siberian Laika
description: The East Siberian Laika is a versatile primitive hunting Spitz from the vast taiga forests of eastern Siberia, used by indigenous peoples to hunt sable, bear, elk, and wild boar, as well as to pull sleds in winter. Developed over centuries in the extreme isolation of Siberia, this breed is more diverse in coat color and size than the other Laika breeds, reflecting the independent breeding programs of the many indigenous tribes that developed it. This rugged, independent breed retains strong primitive hunting instincts and requires an experienced owner who understands its wild, self-reliant nature.

name: West Siberian Laika
description: The West Siberian Laika is Russia's most numerous Laika breed, developed by the Khanty and Mansi peoples of western Siberia as an all-purpose hunting dog for tracking squirrel, sable, bear, elk, and wild boar through the dense boreal forests of the region. This breed is considered the most elegant of the Laika breeds, with a more refined, wedge-shaped head and more consistent white or gray coloring than the East Siberian Laika. The West Siberian Laika's exceptional nose, high prey drive, and endurance in extreme cold make it one of Russia's most valued working dogs.

name: Russo-European Laika
description: The Russo-European Laika is the smallest of the three Laika breeds, developed from the hunting Spitzes of northern Russia and Finland during the Soviet era in the 20th century to create a standardized hunting breed for the vast boreal forests of northwestern Russia. This breed was specifically selected for hunting squirrel, pine marten, and sable — small furbearing animals of great economic value to the Soviet state — as well as elk, bear, and wild boar. The Russo-European Laika's black-and-white coat distinguishes it most clearly from the other Laika breeds and reflects the influence of Finnish Spitz-type dogs in its development.

name: Yakutian Laika
description: The Yakutian Laika is an ancient sled dog and hunting breed from the Sakha Republic (Yakutia) in northeastern Russia, where it has been used by the indigenous Yakut people for thousands of years in some of the coldest inhabited regions on earth. Among the oldest of all sled dog breeds, the Yakutian Laika is depicted in ancient rock art and has been confirmed by archaeological evidence dating back over 8,000 years. Unlike the Siberian Husky and other sled dogs, the Yakutian Laika also functions as an effective bird and seal hunting dog, making it a true all-purpose Arctic working breed.

name: Appenzeller Sennenhund
description: The Appenzeller Sennenhund is one of Switzerland's four Sennenhund breeds, originating in the Appenzell region of Switzerland where it served as a versatile farm dog herding cattle, guarding property, and pulling carts. This lively, self-confident breed is the least commonly seen of the four Swiss mountain dogs internationally, despite its excellent all-around abilities and striking tricolored appearance. Unlike the other Swiss mountain dogs, the Appenzeller carries its thick-boned tail tightly curled over its back in typical Spitz fashion, giving it a distinctive silhouette.

name: Hovawart
description: The Hovawart is an ancient German working breed whose name means 'estate warden' or 'guardian of the farm,' with references to a dog matching its description appearing in German literature as far back as the 13th century. Nearly extinct by the early 20th century, the breed was reconstructed in the 1920s by breeding farm dogs with similar characteristics along with crosses to German Shepherds, Newfoundlands, and Leonbergers. Today the Hovawart is highly valued in Germany as a search and rescue dog, tracking dog, and protection dog, with its keen nose rivaling that of the German Shepherd in scent work trials.

name: Eurasier
description: The Eurasier is a relatively modern German Spitz-type breed created in the 1960s by Julius Wipfel, who crossed the Wolf Spitz, Chow Chow, and Samoyed to produce a balanced, calm companion dog with the beauty of a Northern Spitz and the composed temperament of a family dog. This breed is known for its deep bond with its entire family group and distress when separated from family members for long periods, making it unsuitable as a kennel dog. The Eurasier's remarkable stability, calmness, and moderate exercise needs make it an ideal companion for families seeking a beautiful, low-key dog.

name: Volpino Italiano
description: The Volpino Italiano is an ancient Italian Spitz-type breed that appears in art dating to 1500 BC, and was a beloved companion of Italian nobility and a favorite of Michelangelo. Almost indistinguishable from the early Pomeranian in appearance, the Volpino nearly went extinct in the mid-20th century, with fewer than five individuals recorded in Italy in the 1960s, before a revival program using dogs found on Italian farms brought the breed back from the brink. This lively, spirited little dog was historically used as a watchdog on farms and in carriages, alerting larger dogs to danger with its loud bark.

name: German Spitz
description: The German Spitz is one of the oldest and most influential of all European dog breeds, believed to be descended from ancient Nordic herding dogs brought to Germany by Neolithic peoples, and the ancestor of the Pomeranian and many other modern Spitz breeds. In the 18th century, German Spitzes were exported to England in large numbers and became fashionable companion dogs for Queen Charlotte and Queen Victoria, who is credited with miniaturizing the breed to create the Pomeranian. Available in several size varieties, the Mittelspitz (medium) and Kleinspitz (small) are the most common, with the magnificent Großspitz (giant) and tiny Zwergspitz (dwarf/Pomeranian) at the extremes.

name: Japanese Spitz
description: The Japanese Spitz was developed in Japan in the 1920s and 1930s by crossing various white Spitz breeds including the German Spitz, Samoyed, and other white European Spitz-type dogs to produce a compact, pure-white companion dog with a luxurious, cloud-like coat. Despite its immaculately white appearance, the Japanese Spitz's coat has a non-stick texture that repels dirt and dries quickly after bathing, making it surprisingly easy to maintain. This charming, playful breed is devoted to its family and generally good with children and other pets, making it one of Asia's most successful companion breeds.

name: Hokkaido
description: The Hokkaido, also known as the Ainu Dog or Hokkaido Inu, is one of Japan's six native dog breeds and the oldest, brought to Hokkaido by the Ainu people from Honshu around 3,000 years ago. This powerful, bear-hunting dog was bred to track and bay Hokkaido brown bears, requiring extraordinary bravery and physical toughness to confront one of the world's most dangerous predators. The Hokkaido possesses a particularly strong directional sense that enables it to find its way home over vast distances of unfamiliar wilderness terrain.

name: Kai Ken
description: The Kai Ken is one of Japan's six native breeds and is considered the most pure of all Japanese breeds due to its isolated development in the mountainous Kai province of Yamanashi prefecture, where rugged terrain prevented crossbreeding with other dogs. Distinguished by its stunning brindle coat — unique among Japanese breeds — the Kai Ken was used to hunt deer and wild boar in the dense forests and mountains of Kai. This devoted, one-family dog is sometimes called the 'Tiger Dog' for its distinctive striped coat, and has been designated a national natural monument of Japan since 1934.

name: Kishu Ken
description: The Kishu Ken is a medium-sized Japanese hunting breed from the Kishu region of the Kii Peninsula, where it was used for hundreds of years to hunt wild boar and deer using stalking techniques unusual among most hunting dogs. Unlike most Japanese breeds that flush game by barking, the Kishu hunts in near-silence, stalking its prey and holding it at bay by blocking its escape routes. Today, the Kishu is predominantly solid white — the most preferred color in Japan — and has been designated a natural monument of Japan since 1934, though it remains extremely rare outside Japan.

name: Shikoku
description: The Shikoku is a medium-large Japanese hunting breed from the mountainous Kochi Prefecture on Shikoku island, where the rugged terrain kept it isolated and genetically pure for centuries as it developed into an exceptional deer and wild boar hunter. Resembling a smaller, more agile version of the Akita, the Shikoku is one of Japan's six native breeds and has been protected as a national natural monument since 1937. This athletic, wolf-like breed has extraordinary stamina and agility in mountain terrain, and is sometimes called the 'Japanese wolf' for its wild appearance and independent hunting style.

name: Ryukyu Inu
description: The Ryukyu Inu is an ancient hunting breed from the Okinawa archipelago that has existed on the Ryukyu Islands for thousands of years and has unique anatomical adaptations for climbing trees to hunt wild boar. The breed's remarkable tree-climbing ability — enabled by its non-slip paw pads and strong, curved claws — is unique among Japanese dog breeds and reflects the island ecology where boar would take refuge in trees. Critically endangered with perhaps 400 individuals remaining, the Ryukyu Inu is one of Japan's most rare and precious native breeds, and conservation efforts are underway to prevent its extinction.

name: Jindo
description: The Jindo is South Korea's national dog breed, originating on Jindo Island off the southwestern coast of Korea where it developed in isolation for centuries as a hunting and guarding dog. Famous for its extraordinary loyalty and homing instinct — one Jindo named Baekgu became famous for traveling 300km back to her original owner seven months after being sold — this breed forms an almost singular bond with its primary owner. The Korean government has designated the Jindo as a natural treasure (Natural Monument No. 53) and strictly controls its export, making purebred Jindos rare outside of Korea.

name: Pungsan Dog
description: The Pungsan Dog is North Korea's national dog, originating in the Pungsan region of North Hamgyong province in the mountainous northern part of the Korean Peninsula, where it was bred to hunt Siberian tiger, bear, and deer in extreme cold. This powerful, all-white hunting Spitz is one of Korea's oldest breeds and holds significant cultural importance in North Korea, where it is depicted on postage stamps and gifted to foreign dignitaries as a symbol of national pride. The breed is almost entirely unknown in the West, existing only in North Korea and in small numbers among the Korean diaspora who managed to bring dogs south before the Korean War.

name: Taiwan Dog
description: The Taiwan Dog, also known as the Formosan Mountain Dog, is one of the world's most ancient primitive breeds, descended from the pariah dogs that accompanied the Austronesian peoples to Taiwan some 10,000-20,000 years ago, making it one of the oldest dog lineages on earth. Genetic studies have confirmed the Taiwan Dog is among the most ancestrally pure canines in existence, diverging from the wolf very early in the dog's domestication history and remaining largely unchanged since. These dogs were used by Taiwan's indigenous peoples for hunting wild boar and deer in the mountain forests, and their descendants still roam semi-feral on the island.

name: Indian Pariah Dog
description: The Indian Pariah Dog, also known as the INDog or Desi Dog, is one of the oldest and most primitive dog breeds in the world, unchanged since ancient times and closely related to the earliest domesticated dogs of South Asia some 15,000 years ago. These dogs have survived and thrived for millennia through natural selection rather than human selective breeding, producing an exceptionally disease-resistant, adaptable, and intelligent animal that is believed to represent the original morphology of the domestic dog. The Indian Pariah Dog has been selectively bred in India under the name 'Mudhol Hound' in certain regions, and is now gaining recognition as an excellent adoption candidate due to its health and trainability.

name: Telomian
description: The Telomian is one of the rarest breeds in the world, discovered in the remote rainforests of Malaysia in 1963 by anthropologist Dr. Orville Elliot who observed the dog being kept by the indigenous Orang Asli people in the Telom River valley. The breed possesses a remarkable climbing ability, developed for entering the stilted longhouses of the Orang Asli that are elevated several metres above the ground to prevent snake and predator access. With fewer than 200 purebred Telomians estimated to exist worldwide, this may be among the world's most endangered dog breeds.

name: Carolina Dog
description: The Carolina Dog, also known as the American Dingo, is a primitive breed that roamed feral in the isolated wetlands and forests of the American Southeast for thousands of years, and is genetically one of the oldest and most wolf-like of all dog breeds. DNA evidence suggests the Carolina Dog descended from the dogs that crossed the Bering land bridge with the first Native American peoples some 9,000-11,000 years ago, making it one of the oldest living dog lineages in the Americas. Despite its wild heritage, the Carolina Dog can be successfully domesticated and makes a loyal, gentle companion when raised in a home environment from a young age.

name: New Guinea Singing Dog
description: The New Guinea Singing Dog is one of the rarest and most primitive of all canines, named for its extraordinary howling vocalization that involves harmonics and multi-tonal sounds not produced by any other dog breed or wild canid. Isolated in the highland forests of New Guinea for thousands of years, this breed is closely related to the Australian Dingo and represents one of the earliest branches of the domestic dog family tree. The captive population consists of fewer than 300 dogs, all descended from a handful of animals captured in the 1950s and 1970s, and a small wild population was recently rediscovered in the remote highlands of Indonesian Papua.
